content.text: Conditionals and loops are the executable skeleton of every Python program. They determine which code path runs, how many times a block executes, and when execution should stop — making them the direct translation of any algorithm, policy, or agent strategy into working code.

Control flow is the mechanism by which a Python interpreter decides the order in which statements execute. By default Python runs statements top-to-bottom, one after another. Control flow constructs interrupt that linear sequence:
redirect execution down a chosen branch based on a Boolean test;
repeats a block once for each element in an iterable;
repeats a block as long as a condition holds. Three supplementary keywords —
Python 3.10 added structural pattern matching via

, which extends conditional dispatch to work on the shape and content of objects rather than just equality. All of these constructs are block-structured: indentation, not braces, marks the start and end of each controlled block.

Without control flow, a program can only perform a fixed sequence of operations. Real tasks — classifying a document, retrying a failed API call, processing a stream of messages, walking a graph — all require branching and repetition. Control flow is the layer that turns data and functions into algorithms.
In agentic systems specifically, the agent loop (observe → reason → act → observe) is a
loop. Routing an LLM response to the right handler is an
statement. Iterating over a list of retrieved documents to build a context window is a
loop. Mastering these constructs means being able to implement any agent architecture from first principles, without relying on framework magic.
chains handle arbitrarily many cases with a single read-top-to-bottom flow, eliminating the need for lookup tables in most situations.
loops over iterables are safe by design: they stop automatically when the iterator is exhausted, with no risk of off-by-one errors from manual index management.
loops are the natural encoding of "keep running until some runtime condition changes", which maps directly onto retry policies, polling loops, and agent cycles.
deconstructs complex data structures in one statement, replacing verbose nested
on a loop runs only if the loop completed without hitting
, making "search and report not-found" idioms concise and readable.
loops work the same over lists, generators, files, database cursors, and custom objects — one syntax for all iterable sources.
Deep nesting is hard to read
— more than two or three levels of indented
blocks signals that the logic should be decomposed into named functions.
— an unbounded loop is always one missing
away from a runaway process. Every
must have a clearly visible exit condition.
for loop does not expose the iterator
— if code inside the loop needs to "peek ahead" or consume extra elements, a

— deployment on older runtimes (common in enterprise and cloud functions) means falling back to

as a Boolean. Any object can appear there: the language applies
itself. Everything else is truthy. This means

is falsy) — prefer explicit comparisons in those cases.
clauses are evaluated in order; the first whose condition is true wins. The final
is optional and catches all remaining cases. Python does not fall through to subsequent cases (no

works seamlessly: lists, tuples, strings, generators, files, database result sets, and custom classes alike.
Built-in helpers make common patterns concise:
pairs each item with its index;
produces integer sequences without allocating a list;

messages = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "Hello"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "Hi"},
    {"role": "tool", "content": "{"result": 42}"},
]

# Basic iteration
for msg in messages:
    print(msg["role"], ":", msg["content"][:40])

# enumerate — index + value
for i, msg in enumerate(messages):
    print(f"[{i}] {msg['role']}")

# zip — two iterables in parallel
roles = ["user", "assistant"]
contents = ["prompt", "reply"]
for role, content in zip(roles, contents):
    print(f"{role}: {content}")

# range — counting loop
for attempt in range(5):
    print(f"Attempt {attempt + 1}")

# Iterating with early exit
results = []
for doc in retrieve_docs(query):
    if doc["score"] < 0.6:
        continue           # skip low-relevance
    results.append(doc)
    if len(results) >= 10:
        break              # enough context gathered

loop evaluates its condition before each iteration. If the condition is false on the very first check the body never runs, making it safe to use even when zero iterations is the correct result. The loop runs until the condition becomes false or a
The canonical agent cycle pattern is a

with a turn counter and an explicit completion check. Every production agent loop must cap its iterations: an LLM that keeps requesting tools will otherwise loop indefinitely, consuming compute and cost without bound.
break, continue, and the loop else clause
exits the innermost enclosing loop immediately.
skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps back to the loop condition or next-element check. Both work identically in
clause is Python-specific: it executes after the loop finishes its normal course — when the iterator is exhausted for
, or when the condition becomes false for
run if the loop was exited via
. This makes it perfect for "search and report failure" patterns.
evaluates an expression (the subject) and tests it against a series of
patterns. Unlike a C-style switch, Python's patterns can test the
of an object, destructure sequences and mappings, bind sub-values to names, and guard with arbitrary conditions — all in one clause.
the cleanest way to dispatch on the type of an LLM API response, an event from an async queue, or a parsed tool call. The wildcard pattern

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ToolCall:
    name: str
    args: dict

@dataclass
class TextMessage:
    content: str
    role: str

@dataclass
class ErrorEvent:
    code: int
    message: str

def handle_llm_event(event) -> str:
    match event:
        case ToolCall(name="search", args={"query": q}):
            return f"Searching for: {q}"
        case ToolCall(name=tool_name, args=args):
            return f"Calling tool {tool_name} with {args}"
        case TextMessage(role="assistant", content=text):
            return f"Assistant says: {text}"
        case ErrorEvent(code=429):
            return "Rate limited — back off"
        case ErrorEvent(code=code, message=msg):
            return f"Error {code}: {msg}"
        case _:
            return f"Unknown event type: {type(event).__name__}"

# Sequence patterns
def route_command(tokens: list[str]) -> str:
    match tokens:
        case ["quit"] | ["exit"]:
            return "goodbye"
        case ["help", *rest]:
            return f"Help requested for: {rest}"
        case ["run", script] if script.endswith(".py"):
            return f"Running script: {script}"
        case _:
            return "Unknown command"

content.text: Choosing the right construct avoids logic errors and keeps code readable:
— use when iterating a known, finite collection: tool results, conversation messages, retry candidates, file lines, database rows. Prefer
whenever the termination condition is "ran out of items".

— use when termination depends on runtime state that changes inside the loop: "model said done", "budget reached", "task succeeded", "timeout elapsed". The condition is evaluated before each iteration.
— use for one-time branching based on a value or condition. Prefer
chains when checking the same variable against multiple values.
— use when dispatching on the structure or type of an object with multiple distinct shapes. Cleaner than cascading
— use to exit early when a goal is achieved (found the item, collected enough data, received a terminal event).
— use to skip an iteration that doesn't qualify without nesting the entire body inside an
never executed, meaning the target was not found.
or explicit counter. Avoid deeply nested loops over large data — consider generators, list comprehensions, or library functions like
Below is a production-style agent runner that combines all the constructs covered: a
loop for the agent cycle, a
chain for finish-reason dispatch, and a
statement for routing individual tool names.

from __future__ import annotations
import time
from typing import Callable

ToolRegistry = dict[str, Callable[..., str]]

def run_agent_loop(
    messages: list[dict],
    tools: ToolRegistry,
    *,
    max_turns: int = 15,
    max_tokens: int = 16_000,
    initial_backoff: float = 1.0,
) -> str:
    """
    Run an LLM agent loop until:
    - the model returns finish_reason='stop'
    - turn budget is exhausted
    - token budget is exhausted
    - an unrecoverable error occurs
    """
    turns = 0
    tokens = 0

    while turns < max_turns and tokens < max_tokens:
        # --- Call model -------------------------------------------------------
        try:
            response = call_llm(messages, list(tools.keys()))
        except RateLimitError as exc:
            backoff = initial_backoff * (2 ** min(turns, 6))
            time.sleep(backoff)
            continue   # don't increment turns for rate-limit retries

        tokens += response.usage.total_tokens
        turns += 1

        # --- Dispatch on finish reason ----------------------------------------
        if response.finish_reason == "stop":
            return response.content

        elif response.finish_reason == "tool_calls":
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": None,
                              "tool_calls": response.tool_calls})

            for tc in response.tool_calls:
                match tc.name:
                    case "search":
                        result = tools["search"](query=tc.arguments["query"])
                    case "calculator":
                        result = tools["calculator"](expr=tc.arguments["expr"])
                    case name if name in tools:
                        result = tools[name](**tc.arguments)
                    case unknown:
                        result = f"ERROR: unknown tool '{unknown}'"

                messages.append({
                    "role": "tool",
                    "tool_call_id": tc.id,
                    "content": str(result),
                })

        elif response.finish_reason == "length":
            # model ran out of context — summarise and continue
            messages = summarise_messages(messages)

        else:
            break   # unexpected finish reason

    return f"[Agent terminated: {turns} turns, {tokens} tokens used]"
Example 1 — guard clauses to flatten nesting
Instead of nesting the main logic inside success conditions, return early on each failure case. This keeps the "happy path" at the outermost indentation level.
Example 2 — exponential backoff with structured retry
loop over attempt numbers, combined with
, implements exponential backoff cleanly without external libraries.

A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline needs to: fetch candidate documents, filter by relevance threshold, deduplicate by source URL, and stop once the context window is full. All of this is control flow over a list of results.

MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS = 3000
RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD = 0.65

def build_rag_context(
    query: str,
    retriever,
    token_counter,
    *,
    max_docs: int = 20,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Retrieve candidate documents and assemble a context list
    that fits within the token budget.
    """
    candidates = retriever.search(query, top_k=max_docs)
    seen_urls: set[str] = set()
    context: list[dict] = []
    tokens_used = 0

    for doc in candidates:
        # Filter: relevance
        if doc["score"] < RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD:
            continue

        # Filter: deduplicate by source
        url = doc.get("url", "")
        if url in seen_urls:
            continue
        seen_urls.add(url)

        # Budget check
        doc_tokens = token_counter(doc["text"])
        if tokens_used + doc_tokens > MAX_CONTEXT_TOKENS:
            break   # context window full

        context.append(doc)
        tokens_used += doc_tokens

    return context


# Downstream use in a generation call
context_docs = build_rag_context(
    "What is the agent loop pattern?",
    retriever=vector_store,
    token_counter=count_tokens,
)
context_text = "\n\n".join(d["text"] for d in context_docs)
answer = llm(system_prompt + context_text, user_query)
Modifying a list while iterating over it
Removing items from a list during a
loop skips elements because the list shifts under the iterator. The fix is to iterate over a copy or build a new list.
Missing break in an unbounded agent loop
loop in an agent that never hits
— for example because every branch appends to
and calls the LLM again — will run until the process is killed or runs out of money.
, which may be a valid value. Always use
to mean "if the loop body never executed". In Python it means "loop completed without break". These are different when the iterable is empty (
Shadowing the loop variable after the loop
loop ends, holding the last value. Reading it as if it held a "result" is a logic error when the loop ran zero iterations.
Using range(len(xs)) when you could use enumerate
for i in range(len(items)): item = items[i]
Nested loops with the same variable name
for i in ...: for i in ...:
causes the inner loop to silently overwrite the outer loop variable. Use distinct names like
Always cap agent loops with MAX_TURNS
— define the constant at module level and enforce it in every
guard. Consider adding a wall-clock timeout as a second cap.
Prefer for over while for finite collections
because the iterator handles edge cases (empty collection, modification of the original).
Use guard clauses to reduce nesting
on failure conditions rather than wrapping the happy path in nested
requires mental lookup. Clear names prevent entire classes of bugs.
whose body is more than roughly 15 lines is usually better as a named function called from the loop.
Avoid bare except in loop bodies
— catching all exceptions silently swallows unexpected errors. Catch specific exceptions; log and re-raise or record anything unexpected.
is more readable and handles destructuring in one step.
— for complex agent loops, a one-line comment describing what is guaranteed at the top of each iteration saves debugging time later.

Python loops are not inherently slow, but they do run at interpreter speed — roughly 50–100 ns per iteration in CPython 3.12. For tight inner loops over large collections (millions of items), alternatives like NumPy vectorised operations or generator pipelines are 10–100x faster.
The key performance consideration for agent loops is network and API latency, not Python iteration overhead. A

loop calling an LLM API is bounded by the API round-trip (100–2000 ms), not by Python's loop cost. Optimisation effort should focus on reducing the number of turns and token usage, not on micro-optimising the loop structure.
— replace list-append loops with generator expressions or
functions to avoid materialising large intermediate lists.
Avoid redundant work in loop conditions
every iteration; assign to a variable outside the loop if the length never changes inside.
compose iteration logic without building intermediate lists.
if a and b and c:
, Python stops evaluating at the first
. Put the cheapest or most-likely-false conditions first.
Time complexity of common loop patterns:

attrs.latex: O(n) \text{ — single for loop over n items}
O(n \cdot m) \text{ — nested for loops over n and m items}
O(k \cdot n) \text{ — while loop running k turns, each processing n items}

are useful alongside loops for aggregation and memoisation.
— replaces Python loops over numeric arrays with vectorised C operations; essential when processing embeddings or token count arrays.
— a decorator-based retry library that replaces hand-written

import itertools
from more_itertools import chunked, first_true
import tenacity

# itertools.chain — flatten a list of message batches
all_messages = list(itertools.chain(*message_batches))

# chunked — process documents in batches of 10
for batch in chunked(documents, 10):
    embed_batch(batch)

# first_true — find the first tool call with a given name
search_call = first_true(
    response.tool_calls,
    pred=lambda tc: tc.name == "search",
)

# tenacity — retry decorator (replaces manual while/sleep)
@tenacity.retry(
    wait=tenacity.wait_exponential(min=0.5, max=30),
    stop=tenacity.stop_after_attempt(5),
    retry=tenacity.retry_if_exception_type(RateLimitError),
)
def call_api(prompt: str) -> str:
    return llm(prompt)
Python offers several ways to achieve equivalent results; the table below summarises when each is preferred.
Transform or filter a collection into a new list

Unbounded loops are a denial-of-service vector in any server-side agent. An adversarially crafted prompt that causes an LLM to always request more tools can exhaust CPU, API budget, or context window. The defence is always a hard turn cap and a wall-clock timeout, enforced at the loop level, never relying solely on LLM behaviour.

Conditional branches based on untrusted input deserve extra scrutiny. If an LLM response directly sets a variable that controls which branch executes — for example, the name of a tool to call — validate it against a whitelist before dispatching. An LLM that has been jailbroken or manipulated could otherwise cause the agent to call unintended functions.
+ counter when the counter must not be bypassed.
before dispatching a tool call derived from model output.
, turn count, and token count at loop exit to make failure modes visible in production traces.
— design agent tool calls to be idempotent where possible; if the loop restarts (process crash, retry), re-executing already-completed steps should not cause side effects.
Think of control flow as a road network for your program's execution. An
is a fork in the road: the program picks exactly one path and continues. A
loop is a roundabout with a fixed number of exits — once per element. A
loop is a roundabout whose exit sign lights up only when a condition changes.
is an emergency exit from any roundabout.
skips back to the top of the current lap without completing it. The loop
is the sign at the normal exit that says "you completed all laps without using the emergency exit" — it never shows if you took the
door. Keeping this road-network image in mind makes it easy to reason about what will happen when a condition changes mid-execution.
wrapping a turn counter and completion check; always add a hard cap to prevent runaway agents.
is the right tool for finite, known collections;
is right when termination depends on runtime state.
Guard clauses — returning early on failure — keep the main logic at the outermost indentation level and eliminate deep nesting.
(Python 3.10+) cleanly dispatches on the structure of LLM responses, tool calls, and event types, replacing verbose
was never hit — use it for exhaustive search patterns to make "not found" logic explicit.
Never modify a list while iterating over it with
) or build a new list with a comprehension.
Security: validate any tool name, branch condition, or loop-exit signal derived from model output against a trusted whitelist before acting on it.
For tight numeric loops, move to NumPy vectorisation or generator pipelines; for retry loops, use a library like tenacity rather than hand-rolling the
